title: Building dashboards that stay maintainable
description: Notes on structuring dashboard interfaces so data, filters, and repeated workflows stay understandable over time.
lead: Dashboards are easy to start and hard to keep clear. The real work is not only showing data, but keeping the interface understandable as filters, roles, and workflows grow.

blocks.heading: Start from the workflow
paragraphs: A dashboard should start from the decision or action it supports. Before designing cards and charts, I try to understand what the user checks every day, what needs attention, and what action should happen next.
This keeps the interface focused. Metrics become easier to prioritize because they are connected to real work, not only visual balance.

blocks.heading: Keep data boundaries clear
paragraphs: Maintainable dashboards need clear boundaries between summary data, detailed records, filters, and actions. When everything is mixed in one area, the page becomes harder to reason about and harder to extend.
I prefer predictable sections: high-level status first, then filters, then tables or lists, then detail actions where needed.

blocks.heading: Design for future changes
paragraphs: Most dashboards change after real use. New statuses appear, teams ask for additional filters, and reports need more context.
A good dashboard layout leaves room for growth without forcing a full redesign every time the product learns something new.
